recommendation_types: A DIY Air Purifier That Works
positives: Scrubbed 88.2% of tiny particles (size 0.5 to 2.5 microns) in 10 minutes during testing.
Measured PM1.0 CADR of 943 m³/hr (555 CFM) at maximum fan speed, covering spaces up to 80.5 m² (867 ft²).
It is an air purifier you can build yourself with parts you might already have available at home.
Excellent air cleaning performance for the price — one of the best ratios of performance to money spent.
Superb overall particle filtration performance in large, open-concept areas.
You’ll be choosing the materials, so it’s super customizable and a fun project on its own.
Measured low-noise PM1.0 CADR of 636 m³/hr (375 CFM) at ≤ 45 dBA on lowest fan speed.
Can be assembled in roughly 20 to 30 minutes, making it a fun DIY project.
All parts are readily available on Amazon.
Ideal for garages and workshops due to robust performance and affordability.
Consistent top performance when constructed with high-quality filters and a powerful box fan.
negatives: Requires assembly of four filters and a box fan, adding build time and complexity.
You don’t get the option to add a carbon filter to tackle VOCs and odors.
Ugly and bulky design unsuited for bedrooms or living rooms.
Bulky and awkward to move, limiting portability.
There are no extra features you’ll find on commercial devices, like a child lock or the ability to schedule routines.
It can run loudly and consume a lot of electricity, depending on the box fan you use.
No smart functionality—lacks particle sensors, Wi-Fi, sleep modes, and automatic operation.
Very noisy, producing 51.7 dBA at lowest and 67.3 dBA at highest fan speed.
It is a huge device and not the best looking — it takes up a lot of space.
Produces 55.8 decibels on average, resulting in loud fan noise.

positives: It offers great performance for an air purifier under $200.
VeSync companion app integration allows remote control and scheduling.
You can remove the pre-filter for easy cleaning.
It is relatively quiet — it hits 57.7 dBA at its top speed, and its performance at speed 2 (41.9 dBA) is very good.
The filter has pelleted activated carbon, which is better than carbon-impregnated fabric.
Built-in air quality sensor enables automatic fan speed adjustment.
Measured PM1.0 CADR of 367 m³/hr (216 CFM) at maximum fan speed, covering rooms up to 31.3 m² (337 ft²).
Operates relatively quietly even at higher fan speeds.
The VeSync app unlocks smart modes and routines.
It doesn’t use an ionizer, so it is ozone-free.
It comes with auto mode and app support with the VeSync app.
Built-in U-shaped air inlet resists clogging from pet hair, ideal for heavy-shedding pets.
negatives: Lacks remote display off function without using the app.
Bulky design isn’t suited for tight spaces.
The particle filter is not HEPA grade, even though Levoit initially advertised it as H13 HEPA. This doesn’t affect performance.
Its air quality sensor is infrared, so it needs to be cleaned periodically to stay accurate.
The carbon and particle filters are bonded, so once the carbon becomes full (within 6-12 months), you will need to replace the entire filter.
products.notes: Primary URL failed, fell back to walmart.ca

positives: Auto mode adjusts fan speed based on air quality to optimize energy efficiency.
Scraped 81.5% of small particles (size 0.5 to 2.5 microns) in 10 minutes and removed 99% of both small and large particles in 27.2 minutes during testing.
Estimated annual operating cost of $64.31 when run continuously.
Measured 47.9 decibels on the highest setting, quieter than most home refrigerators.
negatives: The sticker price of $230 is higher than many other purifiers tested.
The unit is bulky and lacks handles, making it difficult to move.
products.notes: Primary URL failed (Best Buy Canada), fell back to Home Depot Canada

positives: It has low running costs thanks to affordable filter replacements and low electricity consumption.
It offers better air cleaning performance at top speed than other small models.
It comes with an onboard particle sensor that is highly responsive and unlocks auto-mode.
negatives: It produces a sort of whistling sound when running at its lowest fan speeds – you might not notice it, but some people will.
The carbon-impregnated layer on the filter won’t make a significant difference against odors and gases.
Speed 2 is just over our 45 dBA quiet performance limit, reaching 46.5 dBA.

positives: Companion app integration via VeSync allows remote fan speed control, scheduling, and filter alerts.
Measured PM1.0 CADR of 617 m³/hr (363 CFM) at maximum fan speed, covering rooms up to 52.7 m² (567 ft²).
Built-in light sensor automatically dims the display in dark rooms.
Measured low-noise PM1.0 CADR of 275 m³/hr (162 CFM) at ≤ 45 dBA on ‘Sleep’ mode.
Three-stage filtration system with a pre-filter, HEPA filter, and pellet-type activated carbon filter.
negatives: Generates up to 61.2 dBA at highest fan speed, making it loud in larger spaces.
Bulky form factor and tall footprint may be difficult to place in tight areas.

positives: Multi-stage filtration system comprising a stainless steel mesh pre-filter, two ‘Bio-Guard’ filters for mold, viruses, and bacteria, an ozone filter, and a combination HEPA/pellet-type activated carbon filter.
Measured low-noise PM1.0 CADR of 311 m³/hr (183 CFM) at ≤ 45 dBA on medium fan speed.
Measured PM1.0 CADR of 548 m³/hr (323 CFM) at maximum fan speed, sufficient for rooms up to 46.8 m² (504 ft²).
Operates quietly at lower fan speeds, making it suitable for overnight use.
Built-in particle sensors automatically adjust fan speed based on real-time air quality readings.
Wi-Fi connectivity via the NuWave Connect app enables remote operation, air quality monitoring, scheduling, and filter replacement alerts.
Unique filtration system keeps costs of ownership low by allowing all filters except the HEPA/Carbon filter to be washed and reused for up to 20 years.
negatives: ‘SLEEP’ mode does not reduce fan speed, requiring manual adjustment to lower noise.
Requires regular cleaning of multiple washable filters every 1–6 months.
Noisy at higher fan speeds, reaching up to 67.3 dBA at maximum.
Lacks remote control via voice assistants despite having Wi-Fi connectivity.

positives: Measured PM1.0 CADR of 414 m³/hr (243 CFM) at maximum fan speed, effective for rooms up to 35.3 m² (380 ft²).
Measured low-noise PM1.0 CADR of 177 m³/hr (104 CFM) at ≤ 45 dBA on ‘Eco’ mode.
Four-stage system (pre-filter, True HEPA filter, carbon filter, and ionizer) with the ionizer able to be switched off to reduce ozone output.
Automatic fan speed adjustment and ‘Eco’ mode use the built-in particle sensor to optimize performance and power consumption.
negatives: Lacks Wi-Fi connectivity and companion app for remote operation.
Produces ozone as a byproduct when the ionizer is active, which can be irritating over prolonged exposure.
Makes up to 61.3 dBA at highest fan speed, which can be disruptive in open-concept areas.
products.notes: Primary URL failed (Best Buy Canada), fell back to PuriLite

positives: You can use it with any smart plug, so you can connect it to your smart home system or set simple automations.
The filter replacements are fairly priced and can last up to 12 months.
It offers excellent performance at top speed, outmatching large units like the Winix 9800 and the Xiaomi Elite.
It looks like a million bucks – I know design is subjective, but I think most people will like the look of the 350.
negatives: It comes with an optical sensor rather than a laser sensor, so you can’t forget to clean it regularly.
There is a big jump in how loud this thing gets when you go from speed 1 (37.6 dBA) to speed 2 (49.4 dBA).
It only has one fan speed below 45 decibels (speed 1) and its performance at this speed is weak (170 m3/h).

positives: It includes wheels and a hidden handle for easy maneuvering.
It has a control-panel lock to prevent accidental shutdowns.
It features an energy-efficient Eco Mode that stops airflow when no pollution is detected for 10 minutes.
It operates quietly and can clean an average-sized room of 300–400 square feet five times per hour on a lower setting.
negatives: It lacks app control and remote functionality.
Its air quality indicator lights use a nonstandard color scheme that can be confusing.
Its one-year warranty on the purifier is short for its price.

positives: Filters can last up to five years before they need to be replaced.
It has a one-dial operation that is easy to use right out of the box.
It comes with 15 lbs of activated carbon with zeolite, which is the largest amount we have seen in a household air purifier.
Its body is made out of metal, so it’s durable but heavy and comes on high quality castor wheels.
negatives: At 61.5 dBA, it gets pretty loud when running at top speed.
At maximum fan speed, it pulls 148 watts. This is on the higher side compared to similar-performing air purifiers.
The amount of carbon in the filter heavily restricts airflow, so it has the same CADR as the much smaller Levoit Core 300.
It has a high initial cost of just over $750 but comes with no smart features, no sensor or app support.

positives: It’s the fastest residential air purifier we tested, ranking 2nd after the commercial beast that is the Smart Air Blast – we estimate a PM1 CADR of 462 cfm.
It features four smart modes, giving you more flexibility based on how much power, noise, or energy consumption you want.
It comes with an onboard air quality sensor and shows real-time particle readings on screen paired with a color-coded LED light at the top of the device.
It is made of high-quality materials and looks great.
negatives: It is not cheap, with a list price just over $800.
It doesn’t come with app connectivity, which some people might expect to get when paying upwards of $800.
Occasionally, you may accidentally open the side covers when using the side handles to lift the ProX, if you need to move it around the house in areas where the wheels may get stuck.

positives: It operates quietly.
It offers a 10-year warranty, including parts and repairs for a decade.
It has custom settings and app control via a dashboard phone app.
negatives: It does not have a carbon filter to remove gases.
products.notes: Primary URL failed (IQAir Canada), fell back to Best Buy Canada

positives: It offers excellent air cleaning power even under the 45 dBA limit we set for quiet performance tests (284 cfm).
It’s lightweight and comes with a handle for easy movement from room to room.
The body is made of lightweight wood, making it more sustainable than traditional plastic air cleaners.
You can use any brand of 20×25 MERV 13 filters, which leads to lower running costs.
You can buy the DIY kit, which is cheaper and includes everything you need to build it at home (except the filters).
negatives: It’s a large device that won’t fit in smaller spaces. But if you like the look of the Luggable, then you can choose the 5-fan version.
It doesn’t come with carbon, so it won’t help with odors and chemical gases.
The lightweight wood looks great, but the body is more delicate than plastic or metal you’ll find in other air purifiers.
It doesn’t come with any smart functions or app support.

recommendation_types: Best Value Air Purifier for Small Spaces
positives: Estimated annual operating cost of $30.36, among the lowest in the tests.
Weighs 2.2 pounds, offering exceptional portability.
Measured average noise level at 36.5 decibels, making it one of the quietest small-space purifiers.
Pulled only 6.6 watts on the highest setting, demonstrating impressive energy efficiency.
negatives: Lacks advanced features such as auto mode or app control.
Scored 4.8 in Air Cleaning Performance, indicating modest pollutant removal capability.
products.notes: Primary URL failed (Levoit Canada), fell back to Walmart Canada

positives: Achieved a score of 8.1 in Air Cleaning Performance and cleared smoky air in 46 minutes.
Offers a competitive price-to-performance ratio with a list price of $100.
Includes a simple interface with a lock feature to prevent accidental setting changes and recommends filter changes every 8 months.
negatives: Measured an average of 43.1 decibels, making it 6.3 decibels louder than the overall winner.
Estimated annual operating cost of $96.33, higher than some similarly-sized purifiers.

positives: Compact design fits on dressers or shelves and suits bedrooms or nurseries.
Measured PM1.0 CADR of 154 m³/hr (91 CFM) at maximum fan speed, covering rooms up to 13.2 m² (142 ft²).
Wi-Fi connectivity and VeSync app integration enable remote control, scheduling, and filter replacement notifications.
Measured low-noise PM1.0 CADR of 85 m³/hr (50 CFM) at ≤ 45 dBA on medium fan speed.
Fairly quiet overall operation, with noise as low as 33.6 dBA at the lowest fan speed.
negatives: Lacks built-in air quality sensor, requiring manual speed adjustments.
Slow air cleaning rate struggles in spaces larger than small bedrooms or dorms.
products.notes: Primary URL failed (Levoit Canada), fell back to Walmart Canada

positives: Features a sensor system that monitors three particle sizes and provides real-time air quality data on-unit.
Estimated annual operating cost of $29.37 due to its filter replacement cycle of once every five years.
Consumes very little electricity, contributing to its low operating cost.
Effectively masked odors using a dedicated odor-neutralizing cartridge.
negatives: Took 76 minutes to clear 99% of particulate matter, slower than many competitors.
List price of $250 is high compared to similar purifiers.
products.notes: Primary URL failed (Canadian Tire), fell back to Shark Canada

positives: Measured low-noise PM1.0 CADR of 214 m³/hr (126 CFM) at ≤ 45 dBA on medium fan speed.
Measured PM1.0 CADR of 391 m³/hr (230 CFM) at maximum fan speed, covering rooms up to 33.4 m² (360 ft²) with an H13 filter.
Compact footprint and interchangeable filter options tailored to specific needs.
Quiet at lower fan speed settings (33.4 dBA) making it suitable for bedrooms.
negatives: Becomes noisy at highest fan speed, reaching up to 62.7 dBA.
Does not include an activated carbon filter in the standard configuration tested, limiting VOC removal.

positives: It can clean a 260-square-foot room with the recommended five air exchanges per hour.
It features a pull-out prefilter that collects pet hair, an H13 HEPA filter, and a carbon activated filter to capture VOCs and odors.
It has a Pet Mode that senses when a pet is on the purifier, quieting the fan speed and air cleaning capacity.
It includes storage space for toys.
negatives: It lacks a built-in heated pet bed.
At less than 10 inches high, its low profile may not react quickly to pollutants higher in the room.
None of my test pets used the purifier as intended, which may reduce its effectiveness.

positives: It can clean a 361-square-foot room, exchanging the air 4.8 times per hour at its highest setting of 53 dB.
It has a back handle for easy portability.
It offers an ionizer to neutralize airborne particles and an Eco Mode that stops the fan after 30 minutes of no pollution detection.
It includes three filters—a prefilter, a deodorization filter, and a True HEPA filter.
negatives: Its air quality indicator light uses Coway’s proprietary color system, which can be confusing.
It lacks app or remote control.
It needs to sit about 8 inches from a wall or furniture.
Its glossy white finish attracts pet hair.

products.canonical_product_name: Brondell O2+ Horizon 5-Stage HEPA Air Purifier
positives: It provides an easy-to-read control panel, a real handle, three speeds, and a timer for one, two, or four hours.
It has a slim, understated design that fits easily in small spaces.
Its four-stage filtration—prefilter, HEPA, activated carbon, and cold catalyst—punches above its weight for pollutant removal.
negatives: It has no internal air sensor or app connectivity.
It is very loud at its highest setting, reaching 80 dB.
It lacks an auto mode.

positives: High efficiency with 99.9% particle removal down to 0.3 microns in one hour
Effectively removes harmful airborne chemicals and VOCs from indoor air
Suitable for large rooms up to 700 sq. ft.
negatives: Replacement filters are expensive
High initial purchase cost
Some users find the power light too bright, which can interfere with sleep
